Basic Effect: How talented a person is at performing.
n/a unless writer also an actor
Rating Scale notes: 90-100 is amazing (with 95+ being generational talent); 80-89 is great; 70-79 good; 60-69 is average; 50-59 is below average; less than 50 is poor, but with a floor of 20, which means no known skill possessed.
Engine Rules: Ignored for non-performing roles. Computation of The Floor [Logic: You cannot be a high-level professional without a baseline of human/creative talent]: Min of (COM + DRM) = (Highest Level I Skill) / 2. Computation of the Ceiling [Logic: You cannot express more creative talent than your technical skills can physically support]: The Ceiling: Max of (COM + DRM) = (Highest Level I Skill + 20

value type: Spectrum from 0 (low distance, relatable neighbor) to 100 (high distance, iconic authoritarian)
Basic Effect: How relateablity a person is (from being like a reliable neighbor or boy next door to being an icon so unapproachable, they are simply 'not like us', which can affect their suitability for genres and subgenres, and potentially for personality-based triggers as well.
Determines "Casting Archetype." A 100-rated actor plays kings, CEOs, and superheroes (The Icon). A 0-rated actor plays the best friend, the struggling parent, or the underdog (The Neighbor).
Defines the "Dialogue Class." 100-rated writing is elevated, sophisticated, and perhaps "smarter" than the audience (think The West Wing). 0-rated writing is colloquial, warm, and grounded.
An evening news anchor needs Authority to be trusted with a crisis; a morning host needs Relatability to be invited into the kitchen during breakfast.
Will affect the genre match of their show, as well as affect their personality.
Will affect the genre match of their show, as well as affect their personality.
Rating Scale notes: 0–20: The Pure Neighbor. Maximum accessibility; zero distance from audience. 45–55: The Balanced Pro. Professional stature with human warmth. 80–100: The Absolute Icon. Maximum authority and distance; "untouchable" stature.

value type: Spectrum from 0 (low worldview, the anti-hero) to 100 (high worldview, the idealist)
Basic Effect: How a person seems to view the world (from being a cynical antihero to an idealist), which can affect their suitability for genres and subgenres, and potentially for personality-based triggers as well.
Does the performance feel cynical and weary (The Anti-Hero) or hopeful and sincere (The Idealist)?
Determines the "Moral Universe." 0-rated scripts have shades of gray and harsh consequences (HBO-style). 100-rated scripts have clear heroes and emotional payoffs (Classic Sitcom/Network Drama).
The difference between Investigative Journalist (0) vs. Human Interest Reporter (100).
Will affect the genre match of their show, as well as affect their personality.
Will affect the genre match of their show, as well as affect their personality.
Rating Scale notes: 0–20: The Anti-Hero. Cynical, weary, and dark; fits "Prestige Cable" vibes. 45–55: The Realist. Pragmatic and balanced worldview. 80–100: The Idealist. Sincere, hopeful, and bright; fits "Network Primetime" vibes.

value type: Spectrum from 0 (low energy density, buoyant) to 100 (high density, piercing)
Basic Effect: How dense the energy of a person is (from light and buoyant to gruff and piercing), which can affect their suitability for genres and subgenres, and potentially for personality-based triggers as well.
100-rated actors have a "stare" that cuts through the screen (think 2008-era Don Draper). 0-rated actors have "bouncy" energy that keeps the scene moving and light.
100-rated writing is dense, heavy, and demands 100% focus. 0-rated writing is breezy, easy to digest, and often uses "Patter" or quick-fire gags.
Will affect the genre match of their show, as well as affect their personality.
Will affect the genre match of their show, as well as affect their personality.
Rating Scale notes: 0–20: The Buoyant. "Bouncy," light, and energetic; keeps scenes moving. 45–55: The Steady Pro. Controlled and reliable energy. 80–100: The Piercing. Heavy, dense, and "staring"; commands total attention.

value type: Spectrum from 0 (low transparency, the mystery) to 100 (high transparenct, the Open Book)
Basic Effect: How vulnerable a person is (from being an unknown enigma to an open book), which can affect their suitability for genres and subgenres, and potentially for personality-based triggers as well.
0-rated actors are "cool" and guarded; the audience wonders what they're thinking. 100-rated actors wear their hearts on their sleeves; every emotion is visible and shared.
Defines "Plot Clarity." 0-rated writers love "Mystery Boxes" and subverting expectations. 100-rated writers are character-first and emotionally direct.
The Objective Reporter (0) vs. The Emotional Pundit (100).
Will affect the genre match of their show, as well as affect their personality.
Will affect the genre match of their show, as well as affect their personality.
Rating Scale notes: 0–20: The Mystery. Guarded, "cool," and internal; audience wonders what they think. 45–55: The Accessible Pro. Open enough to be trusted, but retains professional boundaries. 80–100: The Open Book. Heart on sleeve; maximum vulnerability and shared emotion.

Basic Effect: Will be a factor in their popularity (a calculated score, not given or static); will help draw viewers. Not necessarily positive - it's merely name and face recognizability.
Rating Scale notes: 90+ is a genuine star (with 95-100 beig world superstar); 80+ is either household name or recognizable to more; 70+ is well known to media consumers; 60-69 is considered a big deal only to those who watch their show; 50-59 is maybe a familiar name OR face; less than 50 is only vaguely familiar, but with a floor of 20, which means a completee unknown

Basic Effect: Will be a factor in their popularity (a calculated score, not given or static); will help draw viewers. Not necessarily positive - it's merely name and face recognizability.
Rating Scale notes: 90-100 is stunning to all (with 95+ being generational beauty); 80-89 is superstar good lucks; 60-79 is attractive by most standards; 50-59 is average for the industry, but still quite pleasant; 40 is the floor, meaning their attractiveness is not a positive traits for them.

Basic Effect: Will affect whether performace suffers amid grueling schedule, and determine deasibility of being in multiple projects at once.
Rating Scale notes: 90-100 is incredible energy (with 95+ being inhuman level of stamina); 80-89 is a workhorse; 70-79 dependable; 60-69 is average; 50-59 is below average; less than 50 is poor, but with a floor of 20, which means no known skill possessed.

Basic Effect: High Ego characters demand more screen time and higher salaries. If you have two "High Ego" leads, they trigger "Chemistry Friction" penalties, lowering the quality of their shared scenes.
This is the inverse of "Drama." High Professionalism means they never miss a mark and stay under budget. Low Professionalism means they are the reason for "Development Hell."

Tags will indicate which specific vices are substantial. These ratings can trigger events such as drunk driving, drug overdose, shouting match on an airplane, etc. Triggered events can lead to other triggered events, like relapses, rehabs, broken up marriages, death/retirement. They can also lower acting or writing abilities.
Engine Rules: Trait is hidden but can trigger events. If triggered, the rating will become visible (but in text rather than numerical form)
